3: The world's demand for animal protein (meat, eggs and milk) has increased in recent decades, driven by ongoing rapid urbanization and population explosion (Froldi et al., 2022). As a result, livestock pro- duction utilizes 29% of global agricultural water to meet its feed de- mands (Lahlou et al., 2021). Furthermore, the livestock sector is responsible for emitting 14.5% of global anthropogenic greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (Gerber et al., 2013) and 33% of global reactive ni- trogen (N) emissions (Mueller and Lassaletta, 2020). Beef, pork, chicken, egg and milk production systems are the dominant livestock-related water use and N emission sources, contributing to 88% of livestock-related water use (Chapagain and Hoekstra, 2003), 78% of
livestock-related GHG emissions (Gerber et al., 2013) and 35% of agri- cultural N emissions (Uwizeye et al., 2020).

China is a major producer and consumer of beef, pork, chicken, egg and milk products. The water demand from livestock production was estimated at 47 Gm 3 in 2009 (Zhao and Chen, 2014). In 2012, ruminant meat and dairy products generated 39% of the total agricultural GHG emissions and 25% of the agricultural NH3 emissions in China (Du et al., 2018). Yang (2008) estimated NH3 emissions from livestock in China in 2005 as 8.2 Mt in total, with 25.6% from cattle, 19% from pork and 8% from layer production. In 2020, beef, pork, eggs and milk in northern China accounted for 20%, 9.0%, 17% and 36% of the total domestic production in China, respectively (NSBC, 2021). From 1998 to 2021, annual increase in production of beef was 4.8%, pork at 1.4%, eggs at

3.4% and milk at 54.3% (NSBC, 2021). In 2000, feed, enteric fermen- tation, manure and energy use for milk accounted for 1%, 6%, 3% and 0.2% of the total agricultural contribution, respectively; by 2020, those figures had risen to 5%, 24%, 11% and 3%, respectively, due to the rapidly growing livestock production. However, the green, blue and grey water footprints (WFs) of beef, pork and poultry in China were higher than those in the Netherlands (Gerbens-Leenes et al., 2013). The nitrogen footprint (NF) of beef, pork, poultry, eggs and milk in China was 1.4 times, 2.8 times, 5.8 times, 3.0 times and 2.7 times than that in Germany (Zhai et al., 2023). With rapidly growing animal-production, the impacts of livestock production in China on natural resource use and greenhouse gas emissions have been increased. Water use and N emissions from livestock production systems have become hot-spots requiring mitigation in northern China (Zhang et al., 2019).

Re-establishing diet feed components to increase feed conversion ratio and manure management at the farm level may decrease envi- ronmental emissions. For example, Mekonnen et al. (2019) found that a 36% decrease in the total WF of livestock products (beef, pork, chicken meat, turkey meat, eggs and milk) in the United States from 1960 to 2016 due to the combined effects of increased animal productivity and the increase in yields of feed crops. The lower WF of livestock produc- tion is also strongly linked to feed composition. In general, feed con- centrates have relatively large blue and grey WFs, while crop residues, wastes and roughages have relatively small WFs (Gerbens-Leenes et al., 2013; Lu et al., 2018; Ibidhi and Ben Salem, 2020). The higher quality of the feed concentrates means that less feed is needed per unit of livestock production and that the overall WF of livestock production is lower. The WF of livestock production is also strongly influenced by the feed con- version ratio (Gerbens-Leenes, et al., 2013; Carra et al., 2022). Pigs raised in industrial systems often have a lower WF than those raised in extensive systems in Spain and China (de Miguel et al., 2015; Xie et al., 2020) due to the higher feed conversion ratio in the former. The reason is that animals are raised under confined and controlled conditions, which implies limited freedom of movement and reduced energy con- sumption, so that most calories are used for fattening. For industrial systems, the WF of manure management in milk production ranges from 0.5 to 1.7 per kg fat protein corrected milk (i.e. 4.0% fat and 3.3% protein according to the standard of FAO (2010), FPCM) (Yi et al., 2023).

Feed production and poor manure management were found to be responsible for N emissions at the farm level. The link between crop-livestock production and the reduction of fertilizer use in feed production is an effective approach to mitigate N emissions. Manipu- lation of the diets also reduces N emissions, such as by reducing grazing and increasing the use of maize crops in ruminant livestock systems (Schils et al., 2007). In addition, NH3 emissions might be decreased by approximately 50% when solid floors are replaced with slatted floors, by 24% when cleaning frequency is increased, by 22% when straw is added to bedding materials, and by 61% when solid manure is stored via compaction and covering in dairy farms (Hou et al., 2015; Zhai et al., 2023). A technology to mitigate one source of the emission may affect the other sources. For example, straw cover reduced NH3 emission from liquid dairy manure but increased N2O emission (VanderZaag et al., 2009). In addition, few studies have accounted for multiple gaseous emissions, cumulative effects of N emissions at various stages from a whole farm system, or emissions from multiple animals.

Previous studies often focused on a single WF or NF, and less attention has been given to how to mitigate the WF and NF of livestock production for livestock farms based on farm systems. There have been no detailed LCA-based studies on the coupling of the WF and NF of livestock production, although various LCA studies have accounted for some environmental impacts associated with water consumption and N emissions (e.g., Lu et al., 2018; Payen et al., 2020). In view of footprint-style analysis, this research focused on feed use and manure management at different farming scales to explore their WFs and NFs. The objectives of this study were to (1) compare beef, pork, chicken, egg

and milk farming systems that varied in intensity and amount and types of feeds used in a region in northern China to compare the WFs and NFs of livestock products and (2) evaluate mitigation options to decrease the WFs and NFs. The results could help to understand the environmental impacts of different farming systems at various scales to improve man- agement in reducing the environmental impacts of livestock production.

For example, straw cover reduced NH3 emission from liquid dairy manure but increased N2O emission (VanderZaag et al., 2009). In addition, few studies have accounted for multiple gaseous emissions, cumulative effects of N emissions at various stages from a whole farm system, or emissions from multiple animals.

Previous studies often focused on a single WF or NF, and less attention has been given to how to mitigate the WF and NF of livestock production for livestock farms based on farm systems. There have been no detailed LCA-based studies on the coupling of the WF and NF of livestock production, although various LCA studies have accounted for some environmental impacts associated with water consumption and N emissions (e.g., Lu et al., 2018; Payen et al., 2020). In view of footprint-style analysis, this research focused on feed use and manure management at different farming scales to explore their WFs and NFs. The objectives of this study were to (1) compare beef, pork, chicken, egg

and milk farming systems that varied in intensity and amount and types of feeds used in a region in northern China to compare the WFs and NFs of livestock products and (2) evaluate mitigation options to decrease the WFs and NFs. The results could help to understand the environmental impacts of different farming systems at various scales to improve man- agement in reducing the environmental impacts of livestock production.
7: The world's demand for animal protein (meat, eggs and milk) has increased in recent decades, driven by ongoing rapid urbanization and population explosion (Froldi et al., 2022). As a result, livestock pro- duction utilizes 29% of global agricultural water to meet its feed de- mands (Lahlou et al., 2021). Furthermore, the livestock sector is responsible for emitting 14.5% of global anthropogenic greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (Gerber et al., 2013) and 33% of global reactive ni- trogen (N) emissions (Mueller and Lassaletta, 2020). Beef, pork, chicken, egg and milk production systems are the dominant livestock-related water use and N emission sources, contributing to 88% of livestock-related water use (Chapagain and Hoekstra, 2003), 78% of
livestock-related GHG emissions (Gerber et al., 2013) and 35% of agri- cultural N emissions (Uwizeye et al., 2020).

China is a major producer and consumer of beef, pork, chicken, egg and milk products. The water demand from livestock production was estimated at 47 Gm 3 in 2009 (Zhao and Chen, 2014). In 2012, ruminant meat and dairy products generated 39% of the total agricultural GHG emissions and 25% of the agricultural NH3 emissions in China (Du et al., 2018). Yang (2008) estimated NH3 emissions from livestock in China in 2005 as 8.2 Mt in total, with 25.6% from cattle, 19% from pork and 8% from layer production. In 2020, beef, pork, eggs and milk in northern China accounted for 20%, 9.0%, 17% and 36% of the total domestic production in China, respectively (NSBC, 2021). From 1998 to 2021, annual increase in production of beef was 4.8%, pork at 1.4%, eggs at

3.4% and milk at 54.3% (NSBC, 2021). In 2000, feed, enteric fermen- tation, manure and energy use for milk accounted for 1%, 6%, 3% and 0.2% of the total agricultural contribution, respectively; by 2020, those figures had risen to 5%, 24%, 11% and 3%, respectively, due to the rapidly growing livestock production. However, the green, blue and grey water footprints (WFs) of beef, pork and poultry in China were higher than those in the Netherlands (Gerbens-Leenes et al., 2013). The nitrogen footprint (NF) of beef, pork, poultry, eggs and milk in China was 1.4 times, 2.8 times, 5.8 times, 3.0 times and 2.7 times than that in Germany (Zhai et al., 2023). With rapidly growing animal-production, the impacts of livestock production in China on natural resource use and greenhouse gas emissions have been increased. Water use and N emissions from livestock production systems have become hot-spots requiring mitigation in northern China (Zhang et al., 2019).

Re-establishing diet feed components to increase feed conversion ratio and manure management at the farm level may decrease envi- ronmental emissions. For example, Mekonnen et al. (2019) found that a 36% decrease in the total WF of livestock products (beef, pork, chicken meat, turkey meat, eggs and milk) in the United States from 1960 to 2016 due to the combined effects of increased animal productivity and the increase in yields of feed crops. The lower WF of livestock produc- tion is also strongly linked to feed composition. In general, feed con- centrates have relatively large blue and grey WFs, while crop residues, wastes and roughages have relatively small WFs (Gerbens-Leenes et al., 2013; Lu et al., 2018; Ibidhi and Ben Salem, 2020). The higher quality of the feed concentrates means that less feed is needed per unit of livestock production and that the overall WF of livestock production is lower. The WF of livestock production is also strongly influenced by the feed con- version ratio (Gerbens-Leenes, et al., 2013; Carra et al., 2022). Pigs raised in industrial systems often have a lower WF than those raised in extensive systems in Spain and China (de Miguel et al., 2015; Xie et al., 2020) due to the higher feed conversion ratio in the former. The reason is that animals are raised under confined and controlled conditions, which implies limited freedom of movement and reduced energy con- sumption, so that most calories are used for fattening. For industrial systems, the WF of manure management in milk production ranges from 0.5 to 1.7 per kg fat protein corrected milk (i.e. 4.0% fat and 3.3% protein according to the standard of FAO (2010), FPCM) (Yi et al., 2023).

Feed production and poor manure management were found to be responsible for N emissions at the farm level. The link between crop-livestock production and the reduction of fertilizer use in feed production is an effective approach to mitigate N emissions. Manipu- lation of the diets also reduces N emissions, such as by reducing grazing and increasing the use of maize crops in ruminant livestock systems (Schils et al., 2007). In addition, NH3 emissions might be decreased by approximately 50% when solid floors are replaced with slatted floors, by 24% when cleaning frequency is increased, by 22% when straw is added to bedding materials, and by 61% when solid manure is stored via compaction and covering in dairy farms (Hou et al., 2015; Zhai et al., 2023). A technology to mitigate one source of the emission may affect the other sources. For example, straw cover reduced NH3 emission from liquid dairy manure but increased N2O emission (VanderZaag et al., 2009). In addition, few studies have accounted for multiple gaseous emissions, cumulative effects of N emissions at various stages from a whole farm system, or emissions from multiple animals.

Previous studies often focused on a single WF or NF, and less attention has been given to how to mitigate the WF and NF of livestock production for livestock farms based on farm systems. There have been no detailed LCA-based studies on the coupling of the WF and NF of livestock production, although various LCA studies have accounted for some environmental impacts associated with water consumption and N emissions (e.g., Lu et al., 2018; Payen et al., 2020). In view of footprint-style analysis, this research focused on feed use and manure management at different farming scales to explore their WFs and NFs. The objectives of this study were to (1) compare beef, pork, chicken, egg

and milk farming systems that varied in intensity and amount and types of feeds used in a region in northern China to compare the WFs and NFs of livestock products and (2) evaluate mitigation options to decrease the WFs and NFs. The results could help to understand the environmental impacts of different farming systems at various scales to improve man- agement in reducing the environmental impacts of livestock production.

9: <header>2.1. Data collection from the farming systems </header>

The Baiyangdian region, which is located in Hebei Province in northern China, was selected for this case study in the analysis of the WF and NF for various livestock farming systems. A total of 43 farms in beef, pork, chicken meat, egg and milk production at different scales (based on the numbers of animals) were investigated to collect the data used for the WF and NF analysis. The data collection included detailed infor- mation on the numbers of animals, feed data, farm management (such as cleaning and drinking systems), and manure management (such as housing facility, manure storage, manure treatment), which were from an on-farm survey in 2020 and based on records kept by the farm managers. The information for each farming system can be found in the supplementary materials. Beef cattle were divided into calves, heifers and lactating cows. Pigs were divided into weaner pigs, finishers and lactating pigs. All broilers were growing chickens, while layers were divided into growing chickens and laying hens. Dairy cattle were divided into calves, heifers, and lactating cows. The average lactating period and dry period for cows were approximately 290 days and 75 days per year, respectively.
10: <header>2.1. Data collection from the farming systems </header>

The Baiyangdian region, which is located in Hebei Province in northern China, was selected for this case study in the analysis of the WF and NF for various livestock farming systems. A total of 43 farms in beef, pork, chicken meat, egg and milk production at different scales (based on the numbers of animals) were investigated to collect the data used for the WF and NF analysis. The data collection included detailed infor- mation on the numbers of animals, feed data, farm management (such as cleaning and drinking systems), and manure management (such as housing facility, manure storage, manure treatment), which were from an on-farm survey in 2020 and based on records kept by the farm managers. The information for each farming system can be found in the supplementary materials. Beef cattle were divided into calves, heifers and lactating cows. Pigs were divided into weaner pigs, finishers and lactating pigs. All broilers were growing chickens, while layers were divided into growing chickens and laying hens. Dairy cattle were divided into calves, heifers, and lactating cows. The average lactating period and dry period for cows were approximately 290 days and 75 days per year, respectively.
11: In this study, following the guidelines of the Livestock Environ- mental Assessment and Performance Partnership (LEAP, 2018), the system boundary was the cradle-to-farm-gate life cycle analysis to esti- mate the WF and NF per unit production of beef, pork, chicken, eggs and milk. First, we calculated the WF and NF per functional unit, which was 1 kg of carcass weight for meat, 1 kg for eggs and 1 kg of FPCM. Ac- cording to the International Organization for Standardization's (ISO) guideline 14044, allocation is the partitioning of the environmental impact between different coproducts of a specific process and can be performed based on their biophysical (e.g., mass or energy) character- istics. Hence, for the present study, we choose a mass allocation approach to address the relative footprint impact of feed and livestock products (Ledgard et al., 2019).
12: <header>2.3. Life cycle inventory and livestock systems </header>

Following the LEAP guidelines for large ruminants, pigs and poultry, each farming system was divided into two subsystems: the feed sub- system and the livestock subsystem based on the collected data, as well as from industry experts and scientific articles. The general methodology is described in the following sections.
13: Following the LEAP guidelines, roughage and concentrates used by the beef, pork, chicken, egg and milk farms in line with a life cycle analysis from cradle-to-the-animal-mouth are listed in Table 1. Con- centrates were not defined directly, but were split into their constituents for purposes of specific data analysis. For the beef farming system, concentrates were made of 64% maize grain, 17% soybean meal and 10% wheat bran, based on the related survey data. For the pork farming system, concentrates were made of 60% maize grain, 18% soybean meal, and 11% wheat bran. For the chicken farming system, the concentrates were made of 59% maize grain, 22% soybean meal, and 10% wheat bran, while for the egg farming system, the concentrates were made of

18: Following the LEAP guidelines, the 43 representative animal sub- systems were further classified based on their mortality rates, repro- ductive characteristics and productive parameters. To convert live weight to carcass weight, the following coefficient factors were considered: 40% for beef, 75% for chicken and 63% for pork (Xu et al., 2005). Tables 2 and S1-S5 summarize the life cycle inventories of beef, pork, chicken, eggs and milk.

Beef production. In China, small farms are a major component of the beef cattle industry, and beef cattle farming is mainly concentrated in agricultural areas in northern China (Greenwood, 2021). We selected nine finishing farming systems (rearing and/or fattening) ranging from 45 heads to 500 heads (Table S1). Calves enter the farm until heifers reach a slaughtering weight of approximately 400-500 kg.

Pork production. Pork is produced through various systems in China, ranging from small-sized (0-50 heads) production to large-scale (>5000 head) production pig farms in China. Farms of different scales were considered (Table S2). The cycle begins with the sow's reproduction and ends when the fattened pigs reach a slaughtering weight of 97 kg-125 kg.
Chicken production. The production of chicken meat in China is relatively homogeneous and occurs predominantly under intensive farming systems. Nine representative farm systems were selected,

ranging from a relatively small farm (10,000 per slaughter) to a large intensive farm (140,000 per slaughter) reliant on amounts (Table S3). One-day-old chicks enter the farm, where they are fattened for 40-45 days until broilers reach a slaughtering weight of approximately 2.10 kg.

Egg production. Eggs are produced mainly in a cage-type intensive system, similar to chicken meat production. The two-day-old chicks enter the farm and stay there for 2 months (initiation, rearing and development phases; Table S4). Then, they are transferred to the prel- aying and laying stages where they stay for another 18 months (prel- aying and laying phases), during which 16 kg-23 kg egg per hen is produced.

Milk production. The dairy farming system presents a large hetero- geneity and complexity in China. All the farms in this study are operated in year-round confined housing systems with feed brought-in from various off-farm areas (Table S5). The average milk yield as an FPCM is 8.8 t/cow/year.
19: Following the LEAP guidelines, the 43 representative animal sub- systems were further classified based on their mortality rates, repro- ductive characteristics and productive parameters. To convert live weight to carcass weight, the following coefficient factors were considered: 40% for beef, 75% for chicken and 63% for pork (Xu et al., 2005). Tables 2 and S1-S5 summarize the life cycle inventories of beef, pork, chicken, eggs and milk.

Beef production. In China, small farms are a major component of the beef cattle industry, and beef cattle farming is mainly concentrated in agricultural areas in northern China (Greenwood, 2021). We selected nine finishing farming systems (rearing and/or fattening) ranging from 45 heads to 500 heads (Table S1). Calves enter the farm until heifers reach a slaughtering weight of approximately 400-500 kg.

Pork production. Pork is produced through various systems in China, ranging from small-sized (0-50 heads) production to large-scale (>5000 head) production pig farms in China. Farms of different scales were considered (Table S2). The cycle begins with the sow's reproduction and ends when the fattened pigs reach a slaughtering weight of 97 kg-125 kg.
Chicken production. The production of chicken meat in China is relatively homogeneous and occurs predominantly under intensive farming systems. Nine representative farm systems were selected,

ranging from a relatively small farm (10,000 per slaughter) to a large intensive farm (140,000 per slaughter) reliant on amounts (Table S3). One-day-old chicks enter the farm, where they are fattened for 40-45 days until broilers reach a slaughtering weight of approximately 2.10 kg.

Egg production. Eggs are produced mainly in a cage-type intensive system, similar to chicken meat production. The two-day-old chicks enter the farm and stay there for 2 months (initiation, rearing and development phases; Table S4). Then, they are transferred to the prel- aying and laying stages where they stay for another 18 months (prel- aying and laying phases), during which 16 kg-23 kg egg per hen is produced.

Milk production. The dairy farming system presents a large hetero- geneity and complexity in China. All the farms in this study are operated in year-round confined housing systems with feed brought-in from various off-farm areas (Table S5). The average milk yield as an FPCM is 8.8 t/cow/year.
20: <header>2.4. Water footprint and nitrogen footprint assessment </header>

The water footprint (WF) is presented as cubic meters of water used to produce a product. The water footprint calculation methodology was developed by Hoekstra et al. (2011). For animals and animal products, the water footprint is primarily composed of water used for feed pro- duction, drinking water, cleaning water and any water used in pro- cessing animal production. The WF (green, blue and grey) used here is calculated as the actual and potential water use at the farm level divided by the total yield. Detailed information on crop production is considered in this study, as this calculation takes into account numerous factors (e. g., crop type, location, growing season length, precipitation and irriga- tion during the growing season, temperature and wind speed) to calculate crop evapotranspiration. Moreover, the grey water in farm systems refers to the total amount of freshwater needed to absorb the nitrogen and pesticides that leach into groundwater or runoff into sur- face water. The nitrogen footprint (NF) is expressed as the sum of N losses concerning a food product. The NF has also been calculated for agriculture using the LCA approach (Ledgard et al., 2019). Only active N emissions are included in the N footprint; these emissions were divided into three categories: N lost to water (due to leaching, runoff, and erosion), lost to the air (via emissions of ammonia (NH3), nitrous oxide (N20), and nitrogen oxides (Nox)), and N discharged (discharge of manure into water bodies or landfills) (Ledgard et al., 2019). The sup- plementary information provides detailed information about the envi- ronmental footprint calculation, parameters, and survey/activity data.
21: To assess how changing feed components and manure management affect the WFs and NFs of meat, eggs, and milk, seven scenarios were compared. Baseline scenario: the feed components and manure man- agement currently used in the farms. Scenario 1: For changing the feed component, the feed component from the livestock production system with the lowest WF was used. This hypothesis was based on the fact that replacement with the optimal feed component would not change farm productivity. Scenario 2: For improved housing facilities, the effect of replacing the cement floor (cage floor) with a padded floor (slatted floor) was evaluated. Scenario 3: For improved manure treatment, the effect of replacing composting with household biogas was established. Scenario 4: This was the combination of the improved housing facility with improved manure treatment. Scenario 5: This was the combination of the optimal feed component with improved housing facilities. Sce- nario 6: This was the combination of the optimal feed components with improved manure treatment. Scenario 7: The optimal feed component, improved housing facility, and improved manure treatment were all combined (Tables 2 and 3).

24: <header>2.6. Water footprint and nitrogen footprint normalized method </header>

The criteria importance through intercriteria correlation (CRITIC) method is one of the weighting methods that determines objective weights for criteria (Diakoulaki et al., 1995). It is a multiobjective de- cision analysis method for the logical evaluation of a decision matrix to identify the information contained in the criteria on which the selected alternatives are evaluated. Many studies have used the CRITIC method for the evaluation of various decision-making (Polcyn, 2022; Wang et al., 2023). In the present study, the CRITIC method was used to obtain the weights of the WF and NF and then calculate the comprehensive evaluation rank of each livestock production system. The supplementary information provides a more detailed calculation process.
25: All variables, including detailed feed information, sewage destina- tion, and output (meat, eggs, and milk productivity), were considered to estimate the WF and NF for each farming system using the LCA method. The CRITIC method was utilized to integrate the WF and NF for each farming system, while a comparative analysis of the baseline and various scenarios was conducted to determine the optimal feed composition and manure management strategies in the farming systems.
26: 3.1. Livestock water footprint and nitrogen footprint

The water footprints and nitrogen footprints of beef, pork, chicken, eggs and milk were estimated, and the results are shown in Table 4 and Fig. 1. Fig. 2 presents the normalized index for the WF and NF in each farm for all livestock production. The WF of livestock products varied among the different types of products as well as the scale of the farming systems. Beef had the highest WF of all livestock products (12,585 L/kg), followed by pork (8177 L/kg), milk (3933 L/kg), eggs (2011 L/kg), and

chickens (1501 L/kg) (Table 4). The estimates of beef, eggs and chickens WFs were 32%, 38% and 55% smaller than those global WFs, respec- tively. But the estimates of pork and milk WFs were 89% and 273% larger than those global WFs, respectively (Mekonnen and Hoekstra, 2012). For all livestock products, feeds were the largest contributor to the WF, accounting for 98.1%-99.4% of all water consumption (Fig. 1A). Livestock consumed mostly green WFs, instead of blue and grey WFs, from small farms to large farms. The main drivers of the differences in green, blue and grey WFs were diet composition and feed origin. The green WF of livestock was strongly influenced by maize grain, soybean meal, and Chinese wildrye hay, while the blue WF was mainly affected by soybean meal, wheat bran, and cotton seed. Maize grain and soybean meal in Hebei are produced in rainy seasons. Chinese wildrye hay is mainly produced in rain-fed farming systems (without a blue WF), while soybean in Hebei, wheat bran in Hebei, and cotton seed in Xinjiang are often irrigated and fertilized (with a high blue WF). Despite the high blue WF of concentrate feeds compared with rough- ages, the higher feed quality means that less feed is needed per unit of livestock production, which reduced the WF values. Maize grain in Hebei, soybean meal in Hebei and wheat bran in Hebei were main contributors to grey water in all farm systems. This is mainly due to the large usage of maize, soybean meal and wheat bran in farm systems, which results in higher consumption of fertilizers and pesticides.

As shown in Table 4, beef presented the highest NF, with a total of 491.3 N/kg. This was followed by pork with an NF of 178.4 g N/kg and milk with 116.0 N/kg. On the other hand, chicken exhibited the lowest NF with 30.8 g N/kg, as well as eggs with 43.2 g N/kg. Regarding livestock production processes, the NF of livestock products was domi- nated by ammonia at 53.8-83.5% of the total NF for livestock farms. The second main contributor was N2O emissions at 6.4-17.3%, except pork. NOx-N exhibited the lowest contributor, with 4.1-12.1% for all livestock production, as well as NO-N with 6.1-16.8%.
The main reason for the higher NF from the livestock farms was the much higher ammonia emissions, which were predominantly from manure (Fig. 1B), except for chicken, where there are high ammonia
emissions of up to 41.8% (Table S7). Feed production was also a sig- nificant contributor to ammonia emissions and was associated with losses following the application of N fertilizer to soil.

Beef was the livestock group with the highest N2O-N emissions (31.3 g N/kg), followed by pork (17.1 g N/kg), milk (8.3 g N/kg), eggs (5.5g N/kg) and chickens (5.3 g N/kg). The emissions of N2O-N g N/kg product from beef farms were approximately 6-fold higher than those from chicken farms. The relative participation of each N2O-N emission source for the livestock group was different. Manure was the main driver of N2O-N emissions at 42.3-47.6% for beef, eggs, and milk, while en- ergy was the greatest contributor to chicken and pork, accounting for 55.0% and 52.8%, respectively. The second main contributor was en- ergy at 25.5-34.8% for beef, egg, and milk and feed production at 34.0% and 31.4% for pork and chicken, respectively.

In relation to NO3-N emissions, feed production was the highest contributor, with chicken being slightly higher than beef. The lowest NO3-N emissions were with egg production. Emissions of NOx-N from beef farms were up to 7-fold higher than those from egg farms, which was primarily associated with greater fuel use for feed production. In livestock farms, NOx-N was attributed to the fuel use for off-farm feed production, as well as the energy use associated with on-farm con- sumption. Transportation of feed to livestock farms was also a main contributor to higher NOx-N emissions, which was partly due to the geographic segregation of feed production and livestock production systems.

Manure was the main driver of N2O-N emissions at 42.3-47.6% for beef, eggs, and milk, while en- ergy was the greatest contributor to chicken and pork, accounting for 55.0% and 52.8%, respectively. The second main contributor was en- ergy at 25.5-34.8% for beef, egg, and milk and feed production at 34.0% and 31.4% for pork and chicken, respectively.

In relation to NO3-N emissions, feed production was the highest contributor, with chicken being slightly higher than beef. The lowest NO3-N emissions were with egg production. Emissions of NOx-N from beef farms were up to 7-fold higher than those from egg farms, which was primarily associated with greater fuel use for feed production. In livestock farms, NOx-N was attributed to the fuel use for off-farm feed production, as well as the energy use associated with on-farm con- sumption. Transportation of feed to livestock farms was also a main contributor to higher NOx-N emissions, which was partly due to the geographic segregation of feed production and livestock production systems.
30: 3.1. Livestock water footprint and nitrogen footprint

The water footprints and nitrogen footprints of beef, pork, chicken, eggs and milk were estimated, and the results are shown in Table 4 and Fig. 1. Fig. 2 presents the normalized index for the WF and NF in each farm for all livestock production. The WF of livestock products varied among the different types of products as well as the scale of the farming systems. Beef had the highest WF of all livestock products (12,585 L/kg), followed by pork (8177 L/kg), milk (3933 L/kg), eggs (2011 L/kg), and

chickens (1501 L/kg) (Table 4). The estimates of beef, eggs and chickens WFs were 32%, 38% and 55% smaller than those global WFs, respec- tively. But the estimates of pork and milk WFs were 89% and 273% larger than those global WFs, respectively (Mekonnen and Hoekstra, 2012). For all livestock products, feeds were the largest contributor to the WF, accounting for 98.1%-99.4% of all water consumption (Fig. 1A). Livestock consumed mostly green WFs, instead of blue and grey WFs, from small farms to large farms. The main drivers of the differences in green, blue and grey WFs were diet composition and feed origin. The green WF of livestock was strongly influenced by maize grain, soybean meal, and Chinese wildrye hay, while the blue WF was mainly affected by soybean meal, wheat bran, and cotton seed. Maize grain and soybean meal in Hebei are produced in rainy seasons. Chinese wildrye hay is mainly produced in rain-fed farming systems (without a blue WF), while soybean in Hebei, wheat bran in Hebei, and cotton seed in Xinjiang are often irrigated and fertilized (with a high blue WF). Despite the high blue WF of concentrate feeds compared with rough- ages, the higher feed quality means that less feed is needed per unit of livestock production, which reduced the WF values. Maize grain in Hebei, soybean meal in Hebei and wheat bran in Hebei were main contributors to grey water in all farm systems. This is mainly due to the large usage of maize, soybean meal and wheat bran in farm systems, which results in higher consumption of fertilizers and pesticides.

As shown in Table 4, beef presented the highest NF, with a total of 491.3 N/kg. This was followed by pork with an NF of 178.4 g N/kg and milk with 116.0 N/kg. On the other hand, chicken exhibited the lowest NF with 30.8 g N/kg, as well as eggs with 43.2 g N/kg. Regarding livestock production processes, the NF of livestock products was domi- nated by ammonia at 53.8-83.5% of the total NF for livestock farms. The second main contributor was N2O emissions at 6.4-17.3%, except pork. NOx-N exhibited the lowest contributor, with 4.1-12.1% for all livestock production, as well as NO-N with 6.1-16.8%.
The main reason for the higher NF from the livestock farms was the much higher ammonia emissions, which were predominantly from manure (Fig. 1B), except for chicken, where there are high ammonia
emissions of up to 41.8% (Table S7). Feed production was also a sig- nificant contributor to ammonia emissions and was associated with losses following the application of N fertilizer to soil.

Beef was the livestock group with the highest N2O-N emissions (31.3 g N/kg), followed by pork (17.1 g N/kg), milk (8.3 g N/kg), eggs (5.5g N/kg) and chickens (5.3 g N/kg). The emissions of N2O-N g N/kg product from beef farms were approximately 6-fold higher than those from chicken farms. The relative participation of each N2O-N emission source for the livestock group was different. Manure was the main driver of N2O-N emissions at 42.3-47.6% for beef, eggs, and milk, while en- ergy was the greatest contributor to chicken and pork, accounting for 55.0% and 52.8%, respectively. The second main contributor was en- ergy at 25.5-34.8% for beef, egg, and milk and feed production at 34.0% and 31.4% for pork and chicken, respectively.

In relation to NO3-N emissions, feed production was the highest contributor, with chicken being slightly higher than beef. The lowest NO3-N emissions were with egg production. Emissions of NOx-N from beef farms were up to 7-fold higher than those from egg farms, which was primarily associated with greater fuel use for feed production. In livestock farms, NOx-N was attributed to the fuel use for off-farm feed production, as well as the energy use associated with on-farm con- sumption. Transportation of feed to livestock farms was also a main contributor to higher NOx-N emissions, which was partly due to the geographic segregation of feed production and livestock production systems.
31: For each livestock production system, three mitigation practices (seven scenarios) were modeled to identify the potential for reducing the WF of beef, pork, chicken, eggs and milk. For livestock production sys- items, the brought-in crop feeds varied in their WF per kg DM, and therefore, the effects of changing feed components were evaluated. Overall, the changes in feed components decreased the green WFs of beef, pork, chicken, eggs and milk by 9.9, 18.5, 32.2, 31.4, and 18.7%, respectively (Fig. 3). The decrease in the blue WFs of livestock products was 21.6-70.6%, with the highest reduction achieved with milk pro- duction. The reduction in the blue WF was related to the reduction in the use of the irrigated feed. The grey WFs of livestock products decreased overall, but the grey WF for eggs increased. This was possibly due to a change in the source of soybean meal without a reduction in fertilizer application rates in layer farm systems. These mitigation results illus- trated the importance of improving the feed component to achieve reduced water consumption from livestock production systems.

The S2, S3 and S4 mitigations on livestock production systems involved changes in housing facility and manure treatment, respec- tively. The effect of replacing the cement floor (cage floor) and com- posting with a padded floor (slatted floor) and household biogas would not reduce the WF of livestock production systems because there was a small amount of water consumption (mainly evaporation) during the manure management processes (housing, storage and processing). The combination of feed component changes with housing facility and/or manure treatment (S5-S7) resulted in an overall decrease in the total WF by 15.0-33.2% across the five livestock production systems.

For beef farms, replacing Chinese wildrye hay, maize silage, peanut meal and cotton seed feeds with maize grain was estimated to decrease the green, blue and grey WFs of beef by 9.9%, 57.2% and 1.1%, respectively. This result was mainly associated with the higher yield and lower water consumption in producing the maize grains. Meanwhile, for the pork farms, increasing the maize grain proportion and reducing the soybean meal and wheat bran proportion decreased the green WF by 18.5%, blue WF by 29.8% and grey WF by 20.7%. For chicken farms, reducing feed consumption, instead of changing feed components, effectively decreased the WF without causing a decrease in chicken production. For egg farms, increasing soybean and wheat bran and decreasing maize grains was expected to reduce the green WF by 31.4%
Fig. 1. Relative proportion of each source of water footprint (A) and nitrogen footprint (B) in the production of different livestock products in northern China.

and the blue WF by 21.6%, respectively. For milk farms, changing maize silage, Chinese wildrye hay and maize grain feeds to alfalfa hay and soybean meal reduced the green WF by 18.7%, blue WF by 70.6% and grey WF by 38.0%. Thus, WF reduction could be achieved by using more low-water consumption feeds without causing changes in protein and nutrient supply.

35: For livestock production systems, a reduction in the WF of beef, pork, chicken, eggs, and milk with intensification and mitigation practices coincided with corresponding reductions in the NF (Fig. 4). The reduc- tion in the NF of up to 29.4% could be achieved by the changes in feed components. Housing facility mitigation measures could decrease the NF of beef, pork, and milk farms by 2.1-6.0%, while the NF of chicken raising could be increased by 8.1% for meat farms and 14.2% for egg farms due to the larger NH3-N emission values with slatted floors in layer and broiler farms. Simultaneously, manure treatment greatly decreased the NF of livestock production by 2.2-26.3%. Combining two or three mitigation measures gave an estimated decrease in the NF of up to 60.6% across all livestock production systems.

In all cases, the NF was dominated by NH3-N (mainly from housing facilities and manure treatment). For livestock production systems, an 18.4-82.8% decrease in NH3-N in housing facilities and manure treat- ment processing could be achieved with changes in feed components (S1

scenario) due to a decrease in N loss with livestock manure. Meanwhile, the improved manure management practices in beef, pork and milk also largely decreased NH3-N emissions by 3.9-59.7% by changing the housing facilities and manure treatment methods without considering the effects of feed components (S2, S3 and S4 scenarios). Ledgard et al. (2019) also showed a large decrease in the NF of milk in China with changes in manure management.

37: <header>3.4. Overall reductions in NFs and WFs </header>

As shown in Fig. 5, for all five livestock production systems, the normalized indices of the WFs and NFs under the baseline conditions were lower than those in S1, S5, S6, and S7 scenarios, indicating that the WF and NF could be reduced by changing the feed components and manure treatment methods. For beef, pork and milk farms, the highest WF and NF normalized index occurred in the S7 scenario, which confirmed that the combination of changing feed components, improved housing facilities and manure treatment practices could achieve a sig- nificant reduction in the environmental impacts of livestock production. For chicken and egg farms, S6 was the best scenario due to the higher normalized index in the NF. The improved management practices resulted in an average reduction in the green, blue and grey WFs of livestock production systems of 18.3%, 39.8% and 18.4%, respectively.

41: 4.1. Water footprint and nitrogen footprint implications

The WFs and NFs are good indicators for assessing the efficiency and environmental impacts of livestock production. The results from this study showed that in northern China, the major contributors to WFs were feed consumption in the five livestock production systems, and for NFs, the major contributors included feed and the manure management.

highest WFs and NFs per unit production, followed by pork and milk, Among the five livestock production systems, beef production had the with the lowest occurring for chicken meat and eggs. In northern China, the WFs and NFs for beef, pork and milk were higher than those in other countries and regions. This suggests that the environmental costs of raising and consuming these three animal products are higher than those of raising and consuming chicken meat and eggs.

Further comparing the different livestock systems, pork, by far the most popular animal category in Chinese residents' diets, is not more efficient than chicken, eggs, or milk as measured by the two metrics of WFs and NFs. Interestingly, eggs, the second most popular category in China, are more efficient than beef, pork, and milk in production. Beef is composed of a much lower proportion of the meat supply in China but has a much higher contribution to WFs and NFs, as documented by Eshel et al. (2014).

In China, pork is the first meat in terms of consumption, followed by chicken and beef (Shimokawa, 2015). According to Zhang et al. (2018), the projections of meat consumption in China predict an increasing preference for pork demand, assuming that consumer income will continue to increase. 42: 4.1. Water footprint and nitrogen footprint implications

The WFs and NFs are good indicators for assessing the efficiency and environmental impacts of livestock production. The results from this study showed that in northern China, the major contributors to WFs were feed consumption in the five livestock production systems, and for NFs, the major contributors included feed and the manure management.

highest WFs and NFs per unit production, followed by pork and milk, Among the five livestock production systems, beef production had the with the lowest occurring for chicken meat and eggs. In northern China, the WFs and NFs for beef, pork and milk were higher than those in other countries and regions. This suggests that the environmental costs of raising and consuming these three animal products are higher than those of raising and consuming chicken meat and eggs.

Further comparing the different livestock systems, pork, by far the most popular animal category in Chinese residents' diets, is not more efficient than chicken, eggs, or milk as measured by the two metrics of WFs and NFs. Interestingly, eggs, the second most popular category in China, are more efficient than beef, pork, and milk in production. Beef is composed of a much lower proportion of the meat supply in China but has a much higher contribution to WFs and NFs, as documented by Eshel et al. (2014).

In China, pork is the first meat in terms of consumption, followed by chicken and beef (Shimokawa, 2015). According to Zhang et al. (2018), the projections of meat consumption in China predict an increasing preference for pork demand, assuming that consumer income will continue to increase. This is in line with the trend in Europe (Henchion

44: 4.1. Water footprint and nitrogen footprint implications

The WFs and NFs are good indicators for assessing the efficiency and environmental impacts of livestock production. The results from this study showed that in northern China, the major contributors to WFs were feed consumption in the five livestock production systems, and for NFs, the major contributors included feed and the manure management.

highest WFs and NFs per unit production, followed by pork and milk, Among the five livestock production systems, beef production had the with the lowest occurring for chicken meat and eggs. In northern China, the WFs and NFs for beef, pork and milk were higher than those in other countries and regions. This suggests that the environmental costs of raising and consuming these three animal products are higher than those of raising and consuming chicken meat and eggs.

Further comparing the different livestock systems, pork, by far the most popular animal category in Chinese residents' diets, is not more efficient than chicken, eggs, or milk as measured by the two metrics of WFs and NFs. Interestingly, eggs, the second most popular category in China, are more efficient than beef, pork, and milk in production. Beef is composed of a much lower proportion of the meat supply in China but has a much higher contribution to WFs and NFs, as documented by Eshel et al. (2014).

In China, pork is the first meat in terms of consumption, followed by chicken and beef (Shimokawa, 2015). According to Zhang et al. (2018), the projections of meat consumption in China predict an increasing preference for pork demand, assuming that consumer income will continue to increase. This is in line with the trend in Europe (Henchion

49: 4.1. Water footprint and nitrogen footprint implications

The WFs and NFs are good indicators for assessing the efficiency and environmental impacts of livestock production. The results from this study showed that in northern China, the major contributors to WFs were feed consumption in the five livestock production systems, and for NFs, the major contributors included feed and the manure management.

highest WFs and NFs per unit production, followed by pork and milk, Among the five livestock production systems, beef production had the with the lowest occurring for chicken meat and eggs. In northern China, the WFs and NFs for beef, pork and milk were higher than those in other countries and regions. This suggests that the environmental costs of raising and consuming these three animal products are higher than those of raising and consuming chicken meat and eggs.

Further comparing the different livestock systems, pork, by far the most popular animal category in Chinese residents' diets, is not more efficient than chicken, eggs, or milk as measured by the two metrics of WFs and NFs. Interestingly, eggs, the second most popular category in China, are more efficient than beef, pork, and milk in production. Beef is composed of a much lower proportion of the meat supply in China but has a much higher contribution to WFs and NFs, as documented by Eshel et al. (2014).

In China, pork is the first meat in terms of consumption, followed by chicken and beef (Shimokawa, 2015). According to Zhang et al. (2018), the projections of meat consumption in China predict an increasing preference for pork demand, assuming that consumer income will continue to increase. This is in line with the trend in Europe (Henchion

51: 4.1. Water footprint and nitrogen footprint implications

The WFs and NFs are good indicators for assessing the efficiency and environmental impacts of livestock production. The results from this study showed that in northern China, the major contributors to WFs were feed consumption in the five livestock production systems, and for NFs, the major contributors included feed and the manure management.

highest WFs and NFs per unit production, followed by pork and milk, Among the five livestock production systems, beef production had the with the lowest occurring for chicken meat and eggs. In northern China, the WFs and NFs for beef, pork and milk were higher than those in other countries and regions. This suggests that the environmental costs of raising and consuming these three animal products are higher than those of raising and consuming chicken meat and eggs.

Further comparing the different livestock systems, pork, by far the most popular animal category in Chinese residents' diets, is not more efficient than chicken, eggs, or milk as measured by the two metrics of WFs and NFs. Interestingly, eggs, the second most popular category in China, are more efficient than beef, pork, and milk in production. Beef is composed of a much lower proportion of the meat supply in China but has a much higher contribution to WFs and NFs, as documented by Eshel et al. (2014).

In China, pork is the first meat in terms of consumption, followed by chicken and beef (Shimokawa, 2015). According to Zhang et al. (2018), the projections of meat consumption in China predict an increasing preference for pork demand, assuming that consumer income will continue to increase. 56: <header>4.2. Benefits in reducing the water footprints in livestock production </header>

The large variations in the WFs among different products and indi- vidual farms indicated a potential in decreasing the water consumption through a proper combination of management choices and technical solutions already available. Based on the individual farms investigated, the quantification of the WFs showed that the changing in the compo- sitions of feed raw materials could significantly influence the WFs of livestock products. Due to the large proportion of the WFs for livestock production coming from feed, the changes in manure management practices had a very small effect on the values of the WFs. The results were similar to those of other studies; feed is the main contributor to WFs in livestock production (Carra et al., 2022; de Miguel et al., 2015; Mekonnen et al., 2019), including feed composition, feed conversion rations and total feed requirements. Compared with the water con- sumption in feed production, direct water use in animal farms (drinking water and service water) is often negligible. Grossi et al. (2022) reported

that cooling operations had a low incidence (0.04%) on the overall milk WF and that the water used to cool cattle does not impact milk's WF. The environmental impact of different feed production differs widely; thus, optimizing the feed composition would have the potential to reduce the environmental impacts.

Studies that have estimated the WFs of livestock production through optimal feed composition are very limited because the feed composi- tions vary among the different livestock categories. From the perspective of water resource conservation, the most fundamental way to reduce the WF of pork is to reduce the WF of feed crops. For beef and pork, replacing high-water-consumption feed (wheat bran, soybean meal, cotton seed, and Chinese wildrye hay) with low-water-consumption maize grain could reduce the WFs of beef and pork. The replacement of high-water-consumption Chinese wildrye hay with low-water- consumption maize grain and maize silage could reduce the WF of milk. Sultana et al. (2014) reported byproducts have the lowest water consumption. Grass and forage are mainly produced in rain-fed farming systems (low blue WF), while concentrate feeds are often irrigated and fertilized (high blue WF) (Ibidhi and Ben Salem, 2020). However, Chi- nese wildrye hay, wheat bran, soybean meal, and cotton seed have higher WFs in the current study because the wheat, soybean, and cotton used in the farms in this study were grown in water-scarce regions and

mostly rely on irrigation for production, and Chinese wildrye hay has a lower yield than maize grain. Thus, high-yield and low-water-consumption feed crops should be adopted.

Improving feed conversion ratios or reducing total feed requirements are also effective ways to reduce the WFs of beef, chicken and eggs. The optimal WFs of eggs were the lowest compared with the values from New Zealand, the USA and the EU, while the optimal WFs of beef, pork, chicken and milk from the current study were still higher than those in New Zealand, the USA and the EU (Mekonnen and Hoekstra, 2010, 2012). Therefore, improving crop water productivity, selecting high-yielding and water-saving cultivars, vigorously developing agri- cultural water-saving technologies, adjusting suitable crop planting structures and improving feed conversion ratios would all benefit the reduction in the WFs of livestock production (Ji et al., 2022; Ibidhi and Ben Salem, 2020; Xie et al., 2020).

mostly rely on irrigation for production, and Chinese wildrye hay has a lower yield than maize grain. Thus, high-yield and low-water-consumption feed crops should be adopted.

Improving feed conversion ratios or reducing total feed requirements are also effective ways to reduce the WFs of beef, chicken and eggs. The optimal WFs of eggs were the lowest compared with the values from New Zealand, the USA and the EU, while the optimal WFs of beef, pork, chicken and milk from the current study were still higher than those in New Zealand, the USA and the EU (Mekonnen and Hoekstra, 2010, 2012). Therefore, improving crop water productivity, selecting high-yielding and water-saving cultivars, vigorously developing agri- cultural water-saving technologies, adjusting suitable crop planting structures and improving feed conversion ratios would all benefit the reduction in the WFs of livestock production (Ji et al., 2022; Ibidhi and Ben Salem, 2020; Xie et al., 2020).
64: <header>4.3. Benefits of reducing the nitrogen footprint in livestock production </header>

In 2010, China made up 13.8% of the 185 Tg-N of the annual global food NF estimated as losses in agriculture worldwide (Bodirsky et al., 2014). The results from this study indicated that ammonia emissions (>50%) from feed, housing facilities and manure treatment were the main sources of N loss in all livestock farming systems, followed by N leaching from soil to the hydrosphere, which was consistent with the results from other studies (Rotz et al., 2014, 2021; Veltman et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2021). Averaged over all dairy farms in the U.S., 42% of total NH3 emissions came from housing facilities, 30% from manure storages, 25% from field application of manure and 3% from pasture- land (Rotz et al., 2021).

Reducing the NFs of livestock production from the current study could be achieved at three levels: feed composition, housing facility and manure treatment. As reported by Uwizeye et al. (2020), feed produc- tion releases approximately 44 Tg N/yr, particularly through synthetic fertilizer application and manure spreading to croplands and manure deposited on croplands. In terms of NF, reducing the use of feeds with high-N-emission factors, such as wheat bran, soybean meal, cotton seed and Chinese wildrye hay, was assessed as the most effective strategy to reduce NF. Generally, the production of maize had the lowest NO3 - N emission factor in all feed production and less N loss through leaching (Chen et al., 2014).

To reduce the NFs in the whole farm, lowering the N contents in feed could reduce the N content in manure, thus reducing the N losses in housing facilities and manure treatment. In line with the results of Montes et al. (2013), Bougouin et al. (2016) and Veltman et al. (2018), reducing dietary protein content substantially reduced NH3 emission intensity on farm systems by more than 20%. Manure management, as the second main source of N emissions, releases approximately 20 Tg N/yr through volatilization, N leaching and biogas to produce energy (Uwizeye et al., 2020). Consistent with expectations (e.g., Ledgard et al., 2019), the padded floor (slatted floor) and household biogas used in the current study reduced N emissions during manure treatment by up to 60.6%. VanderZaag et al. (2008) summarized previous studies using different cover materials on manure and revealed that when installed properly, the reduction in NH3 emission could be achieved by more than 70%.

The NFs of beef, pork and milk in this study were higher than those from New Zealand, EU-27 (Lesschen et al., 2011), and the USA (Rotz et al., 2015; Veltman et al., 2018). The potential to further reduce the NFs in northern China was still greater, and mitigation measures could be further explored and used. This difference could also be related to the different emission factors used among different studies, which directly influenced the values of Nemissions. N emissions from this study included all emissions from housing, storage and subsequent treatment, while N losses from manure treatment were not included in the studies of Lesschen et al. (2011), Rotz et al. (2015), and Veltman et al. (2018), which might increase the difference among different systems.

To increase nitrogen use efficiency and therefore reduce the N losses of livestock production, it is important to optimize the timing of appli- cations and the application rates for fertilizer used in feed production. This should be a balance in reducing leaching and denitrification losses to ensure that conserved nitrogen is utilized in crop uptake rather than lost through alternative channels. Additionally, it is crucial that housing conditions and manure be regulated at low-emission levels by well- managed feed and slurry systems, as well as that N from manure and crop residue be properly recycled (Oita et al., 2020). Thus, incentivizing measures could be applied to the collection, transportation and recy- cling utilization of manure to reduce N emissions (Hendriks et al., 2016; Lauer et al., 2018).

69: The grey WF analysis in this study was focusing on the potential water use related to N losses (e.g. chemical fertilizers and manure management) and pesticides, without including the metals and antibi- otics in raising animals. The aquatic acidification (or ecotoxicity) caused by metals and antibiotics should be included in future research to pro- vide a more thorough evaluation of the effects of livestock production on water quality. Other uncertainties with this study were related to the NF assessment. The composition of concentrates for different sizes of farms was assumed to be the same, and constant N emission factors for fer- tilizer, manure management and energy were assigned, which might influence the NF results from individual farms. The data used were derived from the investigation conducted in 2020, and yearly variations in the feed quantity and compositions might exist for different farms that could cause yearly variations in the NF and WF values. Further studies
that consider these issues might provide a more complete picture of the environmental impacts of livestock production in a region.
70: Increasing the understanding of the environmental performance of current livestock production systems is essential to policy-makers, en- trepreneurs and extension services at the farm level. The results from 43 farms with different livestock production in northern China indicated that there were large variations in WFs and NFs among different farms in producing the same livestock product, and there was greater potential to reduce the WFs and NFs by measures such as reducing the water con- sumption and N emission from feed production, improving housing fa- cilities and manure treatment measures. Optimizing feed composition showed promise in substantially reducing the water consumption embodied in livestock products when low-water-consumption feed (e.g., maize) was used. The LCA analysis indicated that changing the feed composition and the use of mitigation measures could achieve a decrease in WFs by an average of 24.4% and in NFs by an average of 43.2%. This conclusion is a promising finding and warrants follow-up studies in the future and in other regions. Feed composition, feed water requirements, feed origin, and feed conversion ratios were all factors to be considered in reducing the WFs of livestock products. Attention to improving the nitrogen use efficiency of feed production and effectively and reasonably altering manure management practices to avoid N emissions could benefit the reduction in NFs for livestock production.

Other possible pollutants, such as heavy metals, veterinary antibi- otics and carbon footprints, were not considered in this study but may have large impacts as well. This suggests that additional indicators must be considered in optimized livestock production systems. This is an enormous challenge but will yield improved water use and nitrogen pollution control as final results, which are the main aims of 'agricul- tural green development' in China.
